1.name: Qian

lines.meaning: The dragon lies hidden in the water; it is not yet time to act.
The dragon appears in the field; it is favorable to see the great person.

lines.text: All day active, vigilant at night.
lines.meaning: The superior person is active and vigilant all day, and at night he is cautious; though in danger, there is no blame.
The dragon leaps or stays in the abyss; it adapts to the situation, and there is no blame.
The dragon flies in the sky; it is favorable to see the great person.
The dragon flies too high; there will be regret, warning against excess.

1.xiang: Heaven moves with vigor; the superior person strives to strengthen himself unceasingly.

2.name: Kun

lines.meaning: Treading on hoarfrost, the solid ice will come—seeing the small signs, prevent trouble before it arises.
Straight, square, and great; even without learning, nothing is unfavorable—virtue comes from nature.
Holding beautiful talents, one can abide in correctness; or assisting the ruler, not claiming merit, and ending well.

lines.text: Tied sack - no blame, no praise.
lines.meaning: Tying the sack, being cautious in words and deeds; neither blame nor praise.
A yellow lower garment, great good fortune—yellow is the color of the center, symbolizing central virtue.

lines.text: Dragons fight - blood black and yellow.
lines.meaning: Dragons fight in the wilderness; their blood is dark yellow—the extreme of yin leads to conflict with yang, and change arises.

2.xiang: The earth's condition is receptive; the superior person carries all things with deep virtue.

3.name: Zhun

lines.meaning: Hesitating to advance, it is favorable to abide in correctness and to establish feudal lords—in the beginning, it is better to stay than to advance.
Difficulty piles up; the horse turns back. The comer is not a robber but a suitor; the maiden remains chaste and does not marry, only after ten years she marries.
Chasing the deer without a guide only leads into the forest. The superior person sees the opportunity and gives up; going forward brings difficulty.
The horse turns back; if one seeks marriage, it is auspicious, and nothing is unfavorable.
Favor is not widely spread; small matters are auspicious if correct, but great matters forced bring misfortune.
The horse turns back; one weeps blood and tears—the image of being at the end of the road.

3.xiang: Clouds and thunder create difficulty; the superior person brings order out of chaos.

4.name: Meng

lines.meaning: Enlightenment education is favorable for using examples to correct people, freeing them from shackles, but blindly advancing brings difficulty.
Tolerating the ignorant is auspicious. Taking a wife is auspicious. The son can manage the household.
Do not marry such a woman—seeing a rich man, she forgets herself; there is no benefit. This warns against fickleness in learning.
Trapped in ignorance, the situation is difficult—far from a wise teacher, isolated and without help.
A childlike ignorant person is auspicious—humbly seeking instruction brings good fortune.
Enlightening by force is unfavorable; using defensive methods is favorable.

4.xiang: A spring issues from the mountain; the superior person acts with determination and nurtures virtue.

5.name: Xu

lines.meaning: Waiting in the countryside, abiding in constancy brings no blame.

lines.text: Wait on sand - gossip, final good.
lines.meaning: Waiting on the sand, there is some gossip, but ultimately good fortune.
Waiting in the mud, inviting robbers to come—the situation is dangerous, and caution is needed.
Waiting in blood, one escapes from the cave.
Waiting with feasting and enjoyment, abiding in correctness brings good fortune.
Entering the cave, three uninvited guests come; treating them with respect brings ultimate good fortune.

5.xiang: Clouds rise up to heaven; the superior person eats and drinks and enjoys himself.

6.name: Song

lines.meaning: Not prolonging the dispute, though there is some gossip, ultimately good fortune.
Losing the lawsuit, returning and fleeing, the fief of three hundred households is without blame.
Enjoying the old salary, abiding in correctness to prevent danger, ultimately good fortune. Or assisting the ruler, but achieving nothing.
Losing the lawsuit, returning to one's place, changing attitude and abiding in correctness, good fortune.
Deciding the lawsuit, great good fortune—distinguishing right from wrong, fair judgment.
Perhaps receiving a robe due to the lawsuit, but being stripped of it three times in a day—honor gained through litigation is hard to keep.

6.xiang: Heaven and water go in opposite directions; the superior person plans at the beginning of affairs.

7.name: Shi

lines.meaning: The army must have discipline; without it, misfortune.
Being in the army, good fortune without blame; the king bestows multiple commendations.
The army may carry corpses back, misfortune—poor command leads to defeat.
The army retreats and camps, no blame—retreating when knowing difficulty is not wrong.
There are beasts in the field; it is favorable to issue orders, no blame. The eldest son leads the army, but the younger son carries corpses back; even correctness brings danger.
The king rewards merit, enfeoffs states, but should not employ petty people.

7.xiang: Water is in the earth; the superior person nourishes the people and gathers the multitude.

8.name: Bi

lines.meaning: Approaching with sincerity, no blame. Sincerity is like a full earthen vessel; it will eventually attract good things, good fortune.
Approaching from within, abiding in correctness brings good fortune.
Approaching those one should not approach—how sad!
Approaching the worthy outwardly, abiding in correctness brings good fortune.
Open and aboveboard approach; the king hunts on three sides, letting go the animals in front. The people of the town are not alert, good fortune.
Approaching without a leader, misfortune.

8.xiang: Water is on the earth; the ancient kings established many states and treated the feudal lords kindly.

9.name: Xiao Chu
9.judgment: Small accum. Dense clouds, no rain.

lines.meaning: Return to the proper path. What misfortune could there be? Good fortune.
Led back to the proper path. Good fortune.
The spokes burst out of the wagon wheels. Husband and wife roll their eyes.
With sincerity, one avoids fear and danger. No blame.
With sincerity, one binds closely and shares abundance with neighbors.
It rains, then stops. Esteem the fullness of virtue. The woman perseveres in danger. The moon is nearly full. The superior man goes forth—misfortune.

9.xiang: The wind drives across the heavens: the image of THE TAMING POWER OF THE SMALL. Thus the superior man refines the outward aspect of his virtue.
9.meaning: Small accum. Dense clouds, no rain.

10.name: Lu
10.judgment: Tread on tiger tail - not bitten.

lines.meaning: Proceed with simple, honest conduct. No blame.
The path is level and broad. The recluse perseveres—good fortune.
A one-eyed man sees, a lame man walks. He treads on the tiger's tail and is bitten. Misfortune. This is like a warrior acting as a ruler—overreaching.
He treads on the tiger's tail. Fearful and cautious, in the end good fortune.
Resolute action. Perseverance in danger.
Look back at your deeds, examine good and ill fortune. Turning back brings great good fortune.

10.xiang: Heaven above, the lake below: the image of TREADING. Thus the superior man discriminates between high and low, and thereby fixes the aims of the people.
10.meaning: Tread on tiger tail - not bitten.

11.name: Tai

lines.text: Rush grass pulled - others follow. Advance.
lines.meaning: Pull up the reed; its kind follows. Advance—good fortune.

lines.text: Embrace all, cross river, no cliques.
lines.meaning: Embrace the distant, cross the great river. Do not neglect the far-off, do not form cliques. This is the central path.
No level without slope, no going without return. Persevere in difficulty—no blame. Do not worry about sincerity; blessing comes in the midst of daily life.
Lighthearted coming and going. Do not flaunt wealth. Trust without suspicion.
The emperor gives his daughter in marriage. This brings blessing and great good fortune—the noble one acts humbly.
The city wall falls into the moat. Do not send troops. Orders come from the city. Perseverance brings difficulty.

11.xiang: Heaven and earth unite: the image of PEACE. Thus the ruler fashions the course of heaven and earth, furthers the harmony of heaven and earth, and thereby guides the people.

12.name: Pi

lines.text: Rush grass pulled - others follow. Persevere.
lines.meaning: Pull up the reed; its kind follows. Perseverance brings good fortune and success.

lines.text: Embrace and endure - petty prosper, great decline.
lines.meaning: Embrace and comply. The small man gains good fortune; the superior man is blocked. Yet in the end, success.
Bearing shame—the small man gains power but feels inner guilt.
Act on the mandate of heaven—no blame. The multitude will follow and receive blessing.

lines.text: Standstill ends - great person brings fortune.
lines.meaning: End the standstill. The great man gains good fortune. 'Perishing, perishing!'—this vigilance is as firm as a mulberry root.

lines.text: Overthrow standstill - first bad, then joy.
lines.meaning: Overthrow the standstill. First standstill, then joy—when things reach their extreme, they reverse.

12.xiang: Heaven and earth do not unite: the image of STANDSTILL. Thus the superior man withdraws his virtue to avoid danger, and cannot be honored with emolument.

13.name: Tong Ren

lines.meaning: Fellowship at the gate—no blame. Begin unity close at hand.
Fellowship only within the clan—limited, too narrow.

lines.text: Hide weapons on hill. Three years.
lines.meaning: Hide troops in the grass, climb the high ground to watch. For three years, no attack.

lines.text: Mount wall - do not attack. Fortune.
lines.meaning: Climb the wall but do not attack—good fortune. One can restrain oneself.

lines.text: Fellowship - first cry, then laugh. Armies meet.
lines.meaning: Those in fellowship first weep, then laugh. The great army meets in victory.
Fellowship in the outskirts—no regret. Though not the broadest unity, there is no remorse.

13.xiang: Heaven together with fire: the image of FELLOWSHIP. Thus the superior man distinguishes things according to their kinds.

14.name: Da You

lines.meaning: No harmful dealings, no misfortune. Persevere in difficulty—no blame.
Load the great wagon. Going forward—no blame. Accumulation brings fullness.
The duke offers tribute to the Son of Heaven. The small man cannot do this.
Do not flaunt great wealth—no blame.
Sincerity in dealings, both dignified and gentle—good fortune.
Heaven's blessing—good fortune without ill. Follow heaven's way and receive help.

14.xiang: Fire in heaven above: the image of GREAT POSSESSION. Thus the superior man curbs evil and promotes good, in obedience to the good will of heaven.

15.name: Qian

lines.meaning: A modest and yet more modest superior man. With this spirit, cross the great river—good fortune. Extreme humility overcomes danger.
Modesty that is known far and wide. Perseverance brings good fortune.
A superior man with merit yet modest—in the end good fortune. Great labor without pride.
Act on modesty—nothing unfavorable.
Do not oppress neighbors with wealth. When necessary, use force—nothing unfavorable. Modesty is not weakness.

lines.text: Modesty known - correct your own state.
lines.meaning: Modesty known far and wide. Advantageous to lead expeditions against vassal states—great virtue naturally commands influence.

15.xiang: Within the earth, a mountain: the image of MODESTY. Thus the superior man reduces what is too much and increases what is too little, balancing things according to their weight.

16.name: Yu

lines.meaning: Indulging in pleasure and self-satisfaction—misfortune.

lines.text: Firm as rock - do not wait.
lines.meaning: Firm as a rock. Understand before the day ends. Perseverance brings good fortune.
Flattering for ease—regret. Hesitation brings more regret.
He brings joy—great gain. Do not doubt. Friends gather like hairpins.
Persevere against illness. Long-lasting, not perishing—remember danger in joy.
Dark and indulgent in pleasure. If one can change, no blame.

16.xiang: Thunder comes resounding out of the earth: the image of ENTHUSIASM. Thus the ancient kings made music to honor virtue, and offered it with splendor to the Supreme Deity, together with their ancestors.

17.name: Sui

lines.meaning: Changes in officialdom; perseverance brings good fortune. Going out for dealings will yield results.
Clinging to a small man loses the great man—sacrificing the important for the trivial.
Clinging to the great man loses the small man. Following and gaining something; it is beneficial to remain steadfast and correct.
Following brings gain; even with correctness there is danger. With sincerity and alignment with the right path, being open and upright, what disaster can there be?
Sincerity in the path of goodness brings good fortune.
Bound and then released; the king offers sacrifice on the western mountain—obstruction first, then passage.

17.xiang: Thunder within the lake: the image of FOLLOWING. Thus the superior man at dusk goes indoors for rest and recuperation.

18.name: Gu

lines.text: Correct father corruption - son steps forward.
lines.meaning: Correcting the father's faults; having such a son, the father is without blame. Though dangerous, ultimately good fortune.
Correcting the mother's faults; one should not be too stubborn.
Correcting the father's faults; slight regret, but no great misfortune.
Tolerating the father's faults; persisting in this will lead to difficulty.
Correcting the father's faults; receiving praise.
Not serving kings or princes; pursuing one's own lofty goals.

18.xiang: Wind below the mountain: the image of DECAY. Thus the superior man stirs up the people and nourishes their virtue.

19.name: Lin

lines.meaning: Approaching the people with a heart of influence; perseverance brings good fortune.
Approaching the people with a heart of influence; good fortune and nothing unfavorable.
Approaching the people with sweet words; no benefit. If one already knows anxiety, there is no blame.
Approaching the people with sincere closeness; no blame.
Approaching the people with wisdom; this is the demeanor of a great ruler, bringing good fortune.
Approaching the people with honesty and sincerity; good fortune and no blame.

19.xiang: Earth above the lake: the image of APPROACH. Thus the superior man's teaching and thought are inexhaustible, and he embraces and protects the people without limit.

20.name: Guan

lines.text: Childlike observation - no blame for small.
lines.meaning: Observing like a child; for a petty man, no blame; for a superior man, limitation.
Observing through a crack; beneficial for a woman's perseverance—narrow vision.

lines.text: Observe your life - advance or retreat.
lines.meaning: Observing one's own situation to decide advance or retreat.
Observing the glorious virtue of the state; beneficial to become a guest of the king—embracing the world.
Observing one's own actions; for a superior man, no blame—self-reflection.
Observing the actions of others; for a superior man, no blame—learning from others.

20.xiang: Wind moving over the earth: the image of CONTEMPLATION. Thus the ancient kings inspected the regions and observed the people to establish teachings.

21.name: Shi He

lines.meaning: Feet in stocks covering the toes; small punishment as a warning, no blame.
Biting soft meat but covering the nose—applying punishment too deeply, but no blame.
Biting dried meat and encountering poison; slight discomfort, no blame—encountering resistance in law enforcement.

lines.text: Bites dried bone - metal arrow. Persevere.
lines.meaning: Biting dried meat with bone, encountering a metal arrow; perseverance in difficulty brings good fortune.
Biting dried meat and obtaining gold; perseverance to guard against danger, no blame.
Shoulders in a cangue covering the ears; misfortune—accumulated evil unrepented leads to great disaster.

21.xiang: Thunder and lightning: the image of BITING THROUGH. Thus the ancient kings clarified punishments and established laws.

22.name: Bi

lines.meaning: Adorning the toes; abandoning the carriage and walking—valuing substance over form.
Adorning the beard—paying attention to appearance but not neglecting the essential.
Graceful and brilliant like water's moistening; perseverance brings good fortune.
Plain and white; horses come galloping, not as bandits but as suitors.

lines.text: Grace in garden - small gift, final good.
lines.meaning: Adorning in the hills and gardens; the bundle of silk is meager, seemingly shabby, but ultimately good fortune.
Adorning with white; returning to simplicity, no blame—the highest grace is natural simplicity.

22.xiang: Fire below the mountain: the image of GRACE. Thus the superior man attends to ordinary affairs but dares not decide lawsuits.

23.name: Bo

lines.meaning: The bed leg is split; destroying the right path brings misfortune—foundation shaken.
The bed frame is split; destroying the right path brings misfortune—deepening condition.
Splitting apart, but no blame—not losing the right path amid decay.
The bed surface is split, reaching the skin; misfortune—already endangering oneself.

lines.text: String of fish - gain favor below.
lines.meaning: Acting like a favored concubine; nothing unfavorable—responding with gentleness.

lines.text: Great fruit uneaten - noble ride, common lose.
lines.meaning: The great fruit not eaten; the superior man gains a carriage, the petty man loses his roof—good and evil are ultimately requited.

23.xiang: The mountain resting on the earth: the image of SPLITTING APART. Thus the superior man enriches the people below to secure his dwelling.

24.name: Fu

lines.text: Return after short stray - great fortune.
lines.meaning: Turning back after going a short distance; no great regret, great good fortune—timely correction of errors.
Beautiful return; good fortune—walking with goodness.
Frequent returns; though dangerous, no blame—knowing errors and correcting them.
Returning alone to the right path amid the crowd—maintaining integrity alone.
Honest and sincere return; no regret.
Losing the path of return; misfortune, with natural and human calamities. Sending troops to war will end in great defeat.

24.xiang: Thunder within the earth: the image of RETURN. Thus the ancient kings closed the passes on the solstice; merchants and travelers did not move, and the ruler did not inspect the regions.

25.name: Wu Wang

lines.meaning: Do not act willfully; going forward brings good fortune—follow nature and all goes well.
Do not expect harvest from plowing, nor bounty from newly cleared fields; thus proceeding is beneficial—ask not about results, only about effort.
Unexpected disaster—someone ties an ox by the roadside, a passerby leads it away, and the villagers suffer.
Perseverance brings no misfortune.
Unexpected illness heals without medicine—do not interfere with natural recovery.
Though not acting willfully, action still brings disaster, no benefit—when the time is unfavorable, do not force matters.

25.xiang: Thunder rolls under heaven, and all things are in accord with the state of innocence. The ancient kings, in accordance with this, fostered the seasons and nourished all things.

26.name: Da Chu

lines.meaning: Danger, it is beneficial to stop—in the early stage of accumulation, do not act rashly.
The body of the cart separates from the axle—stop advancing and gather strength.
A fine horse gallops; persevere in hardship. Practice daily chariot and guard duties; going forward is beneficial.
A horizontal beam is bound to the horns of a young calf (to prevent injury)—great good fortune—prevent trouble before it arises.
The tusks of a gelded boar bring good fortune—eliminate ferocity and turn danger into safety.
The path of heaven is open and unobstructed; success—great accumulation achieved, unimpeded progress.

26.xiang: Heaven within the mountain: the image of Great Accumulation. Thus the superior man acquaints himself with many sayings and deeds of the past, in order to accumulate virtue.

27.name: Yi

lines.text: Leave your tortoise - watch me eat. Misfortune.
lines.meaning: You forsake your own spiritual tortoise and gaze at me with drooling mouth—misfortune—abandoning your own resources to envy others.
Seeking nourishment upside down, contrary to reason; to act brings misfortune.

lines.text: Offending nourishment - no good ten years.
lines.meaning: Going against the proper way of nourishment; even perseverance brings danger. For ten years, no action is beneficial.
Seeking nourishment upside down yet brings good fortune. Like a tiger staring intently, desire is urgent, but no misfortune.
Contrary to reason; staying still and persevering brings good fortune, but do not cross the great river.

lines.text: Source of nourishment - danger then fortune.
lines.meaning: Nourished by others; danger but ultimately good fortune, beneficial to cross the great river.

27.xiang: Thunder under the mountain: the image of Nourishment. Thus the superior man is careful in his words and temperate in eating and drinking.

28.name: Da Guo

lines.text: Spread white rushes beneath - no blame.
lines.meaning: Spread white rushes as a mat for offerings—no misfortune—extreme caution.

lines.text: Withered poplar sprouts - old man, young wife.
lines.meaning: A withered poplar sprouts new shoots; an old man takes a young wife—nothing unfavorable.
The ridgepole bends—misfortune—excessive strength leads to breakage.
The ridgepole rises—good fortune. But if there are changes, difficulties arise.

lines.text: Withered poplar flowers - old woman, young man.
lines.meaning: A withered poplar blooms; an old woman marries a young husband—no misfortune, no praise.
Crossing water too deep, submerged over the head—misfortune—but courage in facing danger is not blameworthy.

28.xiang: The lake rises above the trees: the image of Great Excess. Thus the superior man stands alone and without fear, and withdraws from the world without regret.

29.name: Kan

lines.text: Abyss doubled - fall in pit. Misfortune.
lines.meaning: Repeatedly falling into danger, sinking into a pit—misfortune—unable to escape from peril.
Danger within danger; only seek small gains—do what is possible in adversity.

lines.text: Forward, backward - abyss. Do not act.
lines.meaning: Danger before and behind, perils upon perils, falling into a deep pit—no action possible.

lines.text: Simple offerings from window - final no blame.
lines.meaning: A jug of wine, two bowls of rice, simple vessels, offered through a window—ultimately no misfortune—in danger, act with simplicity and sincerity.

lines.text: Abyss not filled - danger levels out.
lines.meaning: The pit is not yet full; the danger has subsided—no misfortune.

lines.text: Bound in ropes, imprisoned - three years.
lines.meaning: Bound with ropes, imprisoned in a thicket of thorns, unable to escape for three years—misfortune—trapped in a hopeless situation.

29.xiang: Water flows on and on: the image of the Abyss. Thus the superior man walks in constant virtue and practices the work of teaching.

30.name: Li

lines.text: Step carefully - respect brings no blame.
lines.meaning: Steps are confused yet orderly; respectful caution brings no misfortune.
Yellow clinging—great good fortune—the way of centrality and gentleness.

lines.text: Sunset radiance - sigh at old age. Misfortune.
lines.meaning: Clinging at sunset; if you do not beat the pot and sing, you will sigh in old age—misfortune—accept fate and be free from worry.

lines.text: Sudden arrival - blaze up, die down.
lines.meaning: Sudden arrival, like a blazing fire, like death, like abandonment—comes quickly, goes quickly.
Tears flow like rain, sorrowful sighs, but ultimately good fortune—fear and caution bring good fortune.

lines.text: King uses force - behead leader, capture rest.
lines.meaning: The king goes on campaign, beheads the enemy leader, captures those not of his kind—no misfortune.

30.xiang: Brightness rises twice: the image of the Clinging. Thus the great man continues his brightness and illuminates the four quarters.

31.name: Xian

lines.meaning: Influence in the big toe—the beginning of influence, subtle and stirring.

lines.text: Influence in calf - misfortune. Stay - fortune.
lines.meaning: Influence in the calves—misfortune; staying still brings good fortune—hasty action brings misfortune, quiet waiting brings good fortune.

lines.text: Influence in thigh - holding on brings regret.
lines.meaning: Influence in the thighs, clinging persistently to others; going forward brings difficulty.
Perseverance brings good fortune and regret disappears. Restless coming and going; friends will follow your thoughts.
Influence in the back—no regret—sincere influence without stirring the heart.

lines.text: Influence in cheeks and tongue - chatter.
lines.meaning: Influence in the mouth and jaws—sweet words, promises without substance.

31.xiang: A lake on the mountain: the image of Influence. Thus the superior man receives others with an empty heart.

32.name: Heng

lines.meaning: Seeking deep constancy; even perseverance brings danger, no benefit—haste leads to inconsistency.
Regret disappears—the way of the mean, without bias.
Not constant in virtue brings shame; even perseverance brings difficulty.
Hunting yields no game—not constant in position leads to no achievement.

lines.text: Lasting in virtue - woman good, man dangerous.
lines.meaning: Constant in virtue; perseverance. Good fortune for a woman, misfortune for a man—gentle virtue should be flexible for a man.
Restless and unstable constancy—misfortune—unable to endure.

32.xiang: Thunder and wind: the image of Duration. Thus the superior man stands firm and does not change his direction.

33.name: Dun

lines.meaning: At the tail of the retreat, this is dangerous. One must not wish to undertake anything.
He holds him fast with yellow oxhide. No one can tear him loose.
A halted retreat is dangerous and distressing. To keep people and servants is fortunate.
A voluntary retreat brings good fortune to the superior man, but the inferior man cannot attain this.
A retreat carried out in a friendly manner brings good fortune. Perseverance brings good fortune.
A cheerful retreat brings good fortune in every respect. It is favorable to set forth.

33.xiang: Mountain under heaven: the image of Retreat. Thus the superior man keeps the small men at a distance, not by showing enmity but by his own dignity.

34.name: Da Zhuang

lines.meaning: Power in the toes. Going forward brings misfortune. This is certainly true.
Perseverance brings good fortune.

lines.text: Petty use strength - ram butting fence.
lines.meaning: The inferior man uses power, the superior man does not. If he acts, it is dangerous. The goat butts against a hedge and gets its horns entangled.
Perseverance brings good fortune. Regret disappears. The hedge opens; there is no entanglement. Power depends on the axle of a big cart.

lines.text: Lose goat in field - no regret.
lines.meaning: Loses the goat in the neighborhood. No regret.

lines.text: Ram stuck - cannot advance or retreat.
lines.meaning: A goat butts against a hedge. It cannot go backward, it cannot go forward. Nothing serves to further. If one notes the difficulty, this brings good fortune.

34.xiang: Thunder above heaven: the image of Great Power. Thus the superior man does not tread upon paths that do not accord with propriety.

35.name: Jin

lines.meaning: Progressing, but turned back. Perseverance brings good fortune. If one meets with no confidence, one should remain calm and no mistake.
Progressing, but in sorrow. Perseverance brings good fortune. One obtains great happiness from one's ancestress.
All are in accord. Regret disappears.
Progress like a hamster. Perseverance brings danger.
Regret disappears. Take not gain and loss to heart. Undertakings bring good fortune. Everything serves to further.

lines.text: Advance with horns - attack city. Danger.
lines.meaning: Progressing with the horns is permissible only for the purpose of punishing one's own city. To be conscious of danger brings good fortune. No blame. Perseverance brings humiliation.

35.xiang: The sun rises over the earth: the image of Progress. Thus the superior man makes his virtue bright and luminous.

36.name: Ming Yi

lines.meaning: Darkening of the light during flight. He lowers his wings. The superior man goes forth; for three days he has nothing to eat. He goes somewhere. The host has something to say.

lines.text: Wounded left thigh - saved by horse.
lines.meaning: Darkening of the light injures him in the left thigh. He gives aid with the strength of a horse. Good fortune.
Darkening of the light during the hunt in the south. Their great leader is captured. One must not expect perseverance too soon.
He penetrates the left side of the belly. One gets at the very heart of the darkening of the light, and leaves the gate and courtyard.
Darkening of the light as with Prince Ji. Perseverance furthers.

lines.text: Rose to heaven, fell to earth.
lines.meaning: Not light but darkness. First he climbed up to heaven, then he plunged into the depths of the earth.

36.xiang: The light has sunk into the earth: the image of Darkening of the Light. Thus the superior man lives among the people; he hides his light, yet still shines.

37.name: Jia Ren

lines.meaning: Firm seclusion within the family. Regret disappears.
She should not follow her whims. She must attend within to the food. Perseverance brings good fortune.
When the family is severe, there are sighs and reproaches, but good fortune. When wife and children giggle and chatter, it leads to regret.
She is the treasure of the house. Great good fortune.
As a king he approaches his family. Fear not. Good fortune.
His work is commanded. In the end good fortune comes.

37.xiang: Wind comes forth from fire: the image of The Family. Thus the superior man has substance in his words and duration in his way of life.

38.name: Kui

lines.text: Regret vanishes. Lose horse - do not chase.
lines.meaning: Regret disappears. If you lose your horse, do not run after it; it will come back of its own accord. When you see evil people, guard yourself against mistakes.

lines.text: Meet lord in alley - no blame.
lines.meaning: One meets his lord in a narrow street. No blame.

lines.text: Cart dragging, ox held - bad start, good end.
lines.meaning: One sees his cart dragged back, the oxen halted, a man with a shaved head and a branded forehead. Though at first there is difficulty, the end is good.
Isolated through opposition, one meets a like-minded man with whom one can associate in good faith. Despite the danger, no blame.
Regret disappears. The companion bites his way through the wrappings. If one goes to him, how could it be a mistake?

lines.text: Pig in mud, wagon of ghosts - drop bow.
lines.meaning: Isolated through opposition, one sees one's companion as a pig covered with dirt, as a wagon full of devils. First one draws a bow against him, then one lays the bow aside. He is not a robber; he will woo at the right time. As one goes, rain falls; then good fortune comes.

38.xiang: Above fire, below the lake: the image of Opposition. Thus the superior man, while sharing common ground, differs in his ways.

39.name: Jian

lines.meaning: Going forward leads to difficulties, remaining still brings praise.

lines.text: Official in repeated difficulty - not for self.
lines.meaning: The king's servant is beset by difficulty upon difficulty, but it is not his own fault.
Going forward leads to difficulties; hence he turns back.
Going forward leads to difficulties; hence he turns back and joins with others.
In the midst of the greatest difficulties, friends come to help.
Going forward leads to difficulties; remaining still brings great good fortune. It furthers one to see the great man.

39.xiang: Water on the mountain: the image of Obstruction. Thus the superior man turns his attention to himself and molds his character.

40.name: Jie

lines.meaning: No blame.

lines.text: Capture three foxes - gain yellow arrow.
lines.meaning: One kills three foxes in the field and receives a yellow arrow. Perseverance brings good fortune.

lines.text: Carry burdens and ride - invites bandits.
lines.meaning: A man carries a burden on his back and yet rides in a carriage; this attracts robbers. Perseverance leads to humiliation.
Deliver yourself from your own entanglements. Then others will come to you in trust.
The superior man delivers himself from his bonds. Good fortune. He shows himself trustworthy toward the inferior man.

lines.text: Duke shoots hawk on wall - captures.
lines.meaning: The prince shoots at a hawk on a high wall. He kills it. Everything serves to further.

40.xiang: Thunder and rain set in: the image of Deliverance. Thus the superior man pardons mistakes and forgives misdeeds.

41.name: Sun

lines.meaning: When a task is completed, go forward quickly. No misfortune. But consider the extent of the decrease—timely and moderate.
Perseverance brings good fortune. To undertake something brings misfortune. Not decreasing but rather increasing—not decreasing is itself an increase.

lines.text: Three walk - lose one. One walks - gain friend.
lines.meaning: When three people travel together, one is lost; when one travels alone, a friend is found—seek common ground while reserving differences.
Decrease one's affliction; soon there is joyful news. No misfortune—removing the bad is good.
Someone gives you a tortoise shell worth ten pairs of cowries. Do not refuse. Great good fortune—gain through loss.
Neither decrease nor increase, but rather gain. No misfortune. Perseverance brings good fortune. Going forward brings benefit. One gains a selfless minister.

41.xiang: A mountain above a lake: the image of Decrease. The superior man controls his anger and restrains his desires.

42.name: Yi

lines.meaning: It is favorable to undertake great tasks. Great good fortune. No misfortune—after receiving increase, expand your ambitions.
Someone gives you a tortoise shell. Do not refuse. Eternal perseverance brings good fortune. The king uses it to sacrifice to the Lord. Good fortune.

lines.text: Use fortune for misfortune - no blame. Report.
lines.meaning: Use the increase for relief in times of disaster. No misfortune. With sincerity and holding to the mean, report to the prince with a jade tablet.
Holding to the mean, the prince follows your advice. It is favorable to move the capital—gain trust through sincerity.
Treat others with sincerity and kindness. Without asking, you know it is great good fortune. Your virtue will be repaid with sincerity.

lines.text: No help, some attack - unsettled heart.
lines.meaning: No one helps; instead, some attack. An inconstant heart brings misfortune—when increase reaches its extreme, it reverses; insatiable greed brings misfortune.

42.xiang: Wind and thunder: the image of Increase. The superior man, seeing good, moves toward it; having faults, he corrects them.

43.name: Guai

lines.meaning: The front toes are strong. Going forward rashly without success brings misfortune—do not advance recklessly.

lines.text: Warning calls - armed guards. Dont worry.
lines.meaning: A cry of alarm. Even if there is war at night, there is no cause for anxiety—preparedness prevents disaster.
The cheekbones are strong (anger shows on the face). Misfortune. The superior man resolutely walks alone. Caught in the rain, he is drenched, but though annoyed, no misfortune.

lines.text: No skin on buttocks - difficult walk.
lines.meaning: The buttocks have no skin; one cannot sit or walk steadily. Leading a sheep, regret disappears. But hearing advice, one does not believe it.

lines.text: Decisive as cutting purslane - no blame.
lines.meaning: Resolutely remove the petty person like cutting through a mallow. Holding to the mean brings no misfortune.
No one responds to the cry. In the end, misfortune—the end of the road.

43.xiang: The lake rises up to heaven: the image of Breakthrough. The superior man bestows blessings upon those below and avoids resting on his virtue.

44.name: Gou

lines.meaning: Tie it firmly to a metal brake. Perseverance brings good fortune. Going forward brings misfortune. A lean pig restlessly stirs—the first signs of a petty person must be controlled.

lines.text: Kitchen fish - no blame. Not for guests.
lines.meaning: There is a fish in the kitchen. No misfortune. But it is not favorable to entertain guests—keep the petty person under control.

lines.text: No skin on buttocks - difficult walk.
lines.meaning: The buttocks have no skin; walking is difficult. Danger, but no great misfortune.

lines.text: No fish in kitchen - starting misfortune.
lines.meaning: There is no fish in the kitchen. Undertaking something brings misfortune—losing control of the situation is dangerous.

lines.text: Wrapped melon in willow - hidden beauty.
lines.meaning: Wrap the melon with willow branches. Inner beauty. A star falls from heaven—inner talent, destined by heaven.

lines.text: Meeting at horns - regret, no blame.
lines.meaning: Meeting at the horn. Difficulty, but no misfortune—retreat and hold firm.

44.xiang: Wind under heaven: the image of Encounter. The ruler spreads his commands and proclaims them to the four quarters.

45.name: Cui

lines.text: Sincere but unfinished - chaos. Shout, unite.
lines.meaning: Sincerity cannot be maintained to the end; there is scattering and then gathering. If you call out for gathering, shake hands, and have no anxiety, going forward brings no misfortune.
Being led to gather brings good fortune. No misfortune. A sincere person, even with a meager offering, is favored in prayer.
Gathering but sighing. No benefit. Going forward brings no misfortune, but there is a small difficulty.
Great good fortune. No misfortune—virtue wins the crowd; all look to you.
Gathering and having a position. No misfortune. When trust is not yet gained, long perseverance causes regret to disappear.
Weeping and sighing, tears flowing. But no misfortune—sorrow at not being able to gather is genuine.

45.xiang: The lake rises above the earth: the image of Gathering. The superior man puts away his weapons and guards against the unexpected.

46.name: Sheng

lines.meaning: Sincerely ascending. Great good fortune—rise step by step on a foundation of sincerity.
A sincere person, even with a meager offering, has no misfortune—the heart matters, not the form.
Ascending into an empty city—smooth and unimpeded ascent.
The king offers sacrifice on Mount Qi. Good fortune, no misfortune—act in accord with the time.
Perseverance brings good fortune. Ascending step by step along the stairs—steady promotion.
Darkly ascending. It is favorable to persevere without ceasing—ascend without end, but base it on the right path.

46.xiang: Wood grows within the earth: the image of Ascending. The superior man follows virtue and accumulates small things to become great and lofty.

47.name: Kun

lines.text: Seated on withered tree - dark valley.
lines.meaning: Sitting oppressed on a withered tree, entering a dark valley, seeing no one for three years—the beginning of oppression, deep in difficulty.

lines.text: Distressed by food - red robe approaches. Sacrifice.
lines.meaning: Oppressed by food and drink (excessive feasting). The red official robe just arrives. It is favorable to sacrifice. Undertaking something brings misfortune. No misfortune.

lines.text: Distressed by stone, thorns - no wife.
lines.meaning: Oppressed by stones, leaning on thorns. Returning home, one does not see his wife. Misfortune—no way forward or back.

lines.text: Slow approach - distressed in golden carriage.
lines.meaning: Coming slowly, oppressed by a golden carriage. Difficulty, but in the end, a good outcome.

lines.text: Nose and feet cut - slowly freed.
lines.meaning: Suffering nose-cutting and foot-cutting, oppressed by the red robe. Slowly freed. It is favorable to sacrifice—slowly freed through trials.

lines.text: Tangled vines, restless - regret, then fortune.
lines.meaning: Oppressed by creeping vines, in a state of agitation. Moving causes regret, then regret disappears. Undertaking something brings good fortune—action in oppression brings release.

47.xiang: The lake has no water: the image of Oppression. The superior man stakes his life on fulfilling his will.

48.name: Jing
48.judgment: The well. Town changes, well stays.

lines.text: Muddy well - no one drinks. Old well - no birds.
lines.meaning: Mud in the well; it cannot be drunk. An old well; even birds do not come—if one does not cultivate virtue, no one cares.

lines.text: Well pool shoots fish - jar broken.
lines.meaning: Fish at the bottom of the well can only be shot. The water jar is broken—talent and virtue but not employed.

lines.text: Well clean but not drunk - heart aches.
lines.meaning: The well water is clear but not drunk; this pains the heart. It can be drawn. If the king is wise, all share the blessing.
Repair the well wall. No misfortune—cultivate oneself, awaiting the time of use.

lines.text: Clear well - cold spring water drunk.
lines.meaning: The well water is clear and sweet; cool spring water can be drunk—virtue and talent complete, employed by others.

lines.text: Well open, no cover - great fortune.
lines.meaning: The well is repaired; no need to cover it. Sincerity brings great good fortune—fully open, benefiting all.

48.xiang: Wood above water: the image of the Well. The superior man encourages the people and urges them to help one another.
48.meaning: The well. Town changes, well stays.

49.name: Ge

lines.text: Wrapped in oxhide - cannot change yet.
lines.meaning: Bound with the hide of a yellow ox — at the beginning of revolution, consolidate the foundation and do not act rashly.

lines.text: After the day, change - advance, fortune.
lines.meaning: Only when the day has come does one introduce the revolution. Going forward brings good fortune and no blame — revolution requires waiting for the ripe moment.

lines.text: Advance - misfortune. Discuss change three times.
lines.meaning: Rash action brings misfortune. Perseverance is dangerous. When the proposals for revolution have been discussed repeatedly, one must have sincerity.
Remorse vanishes. With sincerity, change the old mandate. Good fortune.

lines.text: Great person changes like a tiger.
lines.meaning: The great man changes like a tiger shedding its fur. Even without divination, one knows his sincerity — the achievement of revolution is brilliantly visible.
The superior man changes like a leopard shedding its fur; the inferior man merely changes his face. Rash action brings misfortune. To abide in perseverance brings good fortune.

49.xiang: Fire in the lake: the image of REVOLUTION. Thus the superior man sets the calendar in order and makes the seasons clear.

50.name: Ding

lines.meaning: The caldron is overturned, which is favorable for removing stale things. Taking a concubire brings a son. No blame — discarding the old and establishing the new.
There is food in the caldron. My adversary is ill and cannot approach me. Good fortune — having strength, one has nothing to fear.

lines.text: Cauldron handle lost - good food uneaten.
lines.meaning: The handle of the caldron is broken, hindering movement. The delicious pheasant meat cannot be eaten. Rain falls, and remorse vanishes. In the end, good fortune.

lines.text: Cauldron breaks legs - food spills. Misfortune.
lines.meaning: The leg of the caldron is broken, spilling the duke's feast, and the caldron is stained. Misfortune — incompetence leads to failure.
The caldron has yellow handles and golden carrying rings. Favorable for perseverance — with the help of worthy people, the caldron is secure.
The caldron has jade carrying rings. Great good fortune. Nothing unfavorable — hardness and softness complement each other, achieving perfection.

50.xiang: Fire over wood: the image of THE CALDRON. Thus the superior man consolidates his fate by making his position correct.

51.name: Zhen

lines.meaning: Shock comes — terrified and anxious, then laughing and talking freely. Good fortune — fear leads to reflection, which brings good fortune.

lines.text: Shock - danger. Lose valuables - they return.
lines.meaning: Shock comes with violence. One loses a great amount of money. Climb the high hill; do not pursue it. After seven days, it will be recovered.
Shock unsettles one. Acting in the midst of shock brings no harm — moving forward in fear is actually safe.

lines.text: Shock sinks in mud - no blame.
lines.meaning: Shock sinks into the mud — excessive shock leads to stagnation.

lines.text: Shock comes and goes - danger but no loss.
lines.meaning: Shock comes and goes violently, but in the end there is no great loss. Only a fright and an incident.

Shock makes one shrink and tremble, utterly panicked. Setting out on an expedition brings misfortune. The shock does not strike oneself but strikes one's neighbor. No blame. But in marriage, there will be disputes.

51.xiang: Thunder repeated: the image of SHOCK. Thus the superior man, in fear and trembling, examines himself and corrects his faults.

52.name: Gen

lines.meaning: Keeping still in the toes. No blame. Favorable for long-term perseverance — stopping in time at the very beginning of action.

lines.text: Stillness in calves - cannot help. Unhappy.
lines.meaning: Keeping still in the calves. One cannot rescue those he follows; his heart is uneasy — wanting to stop but unable to.

lines.text: Stillness in waist - tears the back.
lines.meaning: Keeping still in the waist. The flesh of the back is torn. Danger chokes the heart.
Keeping still in oneself. No blame — stopping within one's proper bounds, self-restraint.
Keeping still in the jaws. Words are orderly. Remorse vanishes — cautious speech brings no regret.
Stopping with a sincere and generous attitude. Good fortune — stopping at the right place, calm and dignified.

52.xiang: Mountain standing on mountain: the image of KEEPING STILL. Thus the superior man does not let his thoughts go beyond his position.

53.name: Jian

lines.text: Geese approach shore - young in danger.
lines.meaning: The wild goose gradually approaches the shore. The young son is in danger. There are disputes, but no blame.
The wild goose gradually approaches the great rock. Eating and drinking joyfully. Good fortune — resting securely in a stable place.

The wild goose gradually approaches the high plateau. The husband goes on campaign and does not return. The wife becomes pregnant but does not give birth. Misfortune. But it is favorable to defend against robbers.
The wild goose gradually approaches the tree. Perhaps it finds a level branch to perch on. No blame — finding stability amidst unease.

lines.text: Geese approach hill - three years no pregnancy.
lines.meaning: The wild goose gradually approaches the hill. The wife does not become pregnant for three years, but in the end no one can overcome her. Good fortune — perseverance leads to success.

lines.text: Geese approach heights - feathers as ornaments.
lines.meaning: The wild goose gradually approaches the high plain. Its feathers can be used for ritual adornment. Good fortune — noble virtue can serve as a model for the world.

53.xiang: Wood on the mountain: the image of DEVELOPMENT. Thus the superior man abides in virtue and improves the customs.

54.name: Gui Mei

lines.text: Marry as sister - cripple can walk. Fortune.
lines.meaning: The maiden is married as a concubine. Like a lame person who can still walk, going forward brings good fortune — accepting one's proper place brings success.
Seeing with one eye. Favorable for the perseverance of a hermit — though imperfect, one can abide in peace.

lines.text: Marry as handmaid - return as wife. Wait.
lines.meaning: She who should be the principal wife becomes a concubine. She should return and wait — without proper status, words have no effect.

lines.text: Delayed marriage - waiting for right time.
lines.meaning: The marriage is delayed. The delay is due to waiting for the right time — acting when the time is ripe.

The emperor Yi gives his daughter in marriage. Her finery is not as splendid as that of her attendants. The moon is nearly full but not yet full. Good fortune — inner beauty needs no outward adornment.

lines.text: Empty basket, bleeding sheep - nothing good.
lines.meaning: The woman carries an empty basket. The man stabs a sheep but sees no blood. The sacrifice is incomplete — empty and without substance, nothing is favorable.

54.xiang: Thunder over the lake: the image of THE MARRYING MAIDEN. Thus the superior man understands the transitory and knows the defects.

55.name: Feng

lines.text: Meet equal lord - ten days, no blame.
lines.meaning: Meeting a matching host. Even after ten days, no blame. Going forward brings something to celebrate.
In the midst of abundance, one is obscured. At noon, one sees the Big Dipper. Going forward arouses suspicion. Showing sincerity brings good fortune.

lines.text: Abundance darkened - break arm, no blame.
lines.meaning: In the midst of abundance, it is dark as night. At noon, one sees small stars. The right arm is broken. No blame — setbacks in prosperity but no great harm.
Abundance is obscured. At noon, one sees the Big Dipper. Meeting a like-minded person brings good fortune.
Brightness appears. There is celebration and praise. Good fortune — light returns.

lines.text: Abundant house, empty - three years, misfortune.
lines.meaning: The house is large but overshadows the family. Peeping through the door, it is empty and deserted. For three years, no one is seen. Misfortune — when prosperity reaches its peak, pride leads to ruin.

55.xiang: Thunder and lightning come together: the image of ABUNDANCE. Thus the superior man decides lawsuits and executes punishments.

56.name: Lu

lines.meaning: In the journey, one fusses over trivial matters. This brings calamity upon oneself — when traveling, be generous.
In the journey, one finds lodging and carries money. One gains a faithful servant — the image of settling down while traveling.
The lodging is burned, and the servant is lost. Persevere to guard against danger — encountering misfortune on the journey.
In the journey, one finds a place to stay and obtains money and tools, but the heart is still unhappy — a wanderer may find shelter but not peace of mind.

lines.text: Shoot pheasant - lose arrow, final praise.
lines.meaning: Shooting a pheasant, one arrow hits. In the end, one gains praise and a position — achieving something while traveling.

lines.text: Bird burns nest - first laugh, then weep. Misfortune.
lines.meaning: The bird's nest is burned. The wanderer first laughs, then wails. The ox is lost in the field. Misfortune — pride leads to joy turning to sorrow.

56.xiang: Fire on the mountain: the image of THE WANDERER. Thus the superior man is clear and cautious in imposing punishments and does not prolong lawsuits.

57.name: Xun

lines.meaning: Advancing and retreating, hesitating. It furthers one to be like a warrior who is steadfast. — Excessive gentleness leads to loss of footing; resolute action is needed.

lines.text: Prostrate beneath bed - shaman ritual. Fortune.
lines.meaning: Bending lowly under the bed, like a shaman or priest praying frequently. Good fortune, no blame. — Sincerity moves the heart.
Repeatedly following, there is difficulty. — Excessive compliance leads to obstruction.

lines.text: Regret vanishes - three kinds of game.
lines.meaning: Remorse vanishes. In hunting, one catches three kinds of game. — Through gentleness, one gains abundance.

Perseverance brings good fortune, remorse vanishes. Nothing that does not further. Beginning may be difficult, but the end bears fruit. Proclaim orders three days before and three days after the day of Geng. Good fortune.

lines.text: Prostrate beneath bed - lose wealth. Danger.
lines.meaning: Bending under the bed, too humble. Even the tools and money are lost. Perseverance brings danger. — Excessive humility loses the foundation.

57.xiang: The Gentle, the Penetrating. The superior man, in accordance with this, repeats his commands, and carries out his undertakings.

58.name: Dui

lines.meaning: Being friendly and cheerful, good fortune. — Peaceful joy, from the heart.
Sincere and joyful, good fortune, remorse vanishes. — Genuine joy.
Coming to seek joy, misfortune. — Deliberate flattery, impure motives bring evil.

lines.text: Joyous negotiation unsettled - illness brings joy.
lines.meaning: Negotiating joy, not yet settled. Cutting off minor ailments brings happy news.
Sincerity is being eroded by petty men, danger. — Beware of petty men tempting with joy.
Leading others to joy. — Guide with joy, but maintain proper measure.

58.xiang: The Joyous, the Lake. The superior man, in accordance with this, discusses with friends and practices.

59.name: Huan

lines.text: Swift rescue with strong horse - fortune.
lines.meaning: Using a strong horse to rescue, good fortune. — Swiftly remedy at the onset of dispersion.

lines.text: Scattered - hasten to the stand. Regret gone.
lines.meaning: In dispersion, rushing to a place of support, remorse vanishes. — Finding a refuge.
Dispersion of the self, no regret. — Letting go of ego enables gathering.
Dispersion of small groups, great good fortune. After dispersion, gathering into a great multitude like a hill, beyond ordinary imagination.

lines.text: Scattering the great - sweat the proclamation.
lines.meaning: Issuing great commands like sweat, dispersing the accumulated evils of the king's dwelling, no blame. — Removing deep-rooted abuses.

lines.text: Scattering blood - depart far. No blame.
lines.meaning: Dispersion of worries, escaping harm, no blame. — Completely freeing from fear.

59.xiang: The Wind over the Water, Dispersion. The ancient kings, in accordance with this, presented offerings to the Lord and established temples.

60.name: Jie

lines.meaning: Not going out of the door and courtyard, no blame. — Knowing limitation from the start.
Not going out of the door and courtyard, misfortune. — Excessive limitation is harmful; not going out when needed brings evil.
Unable to limit, one sighs, but no blame. — Lack of limitation leads to regret.
Content with limitation, success. — Following limits with ease.
Limiting with sweet moderation, good fortune. Going forward brings merit. — The best limitation is moderate and pleasant.
Harsh limitation, even perseverance brings danger. But knowing regret, danger vanishes. — Limitation must not be excessive.

60.xiang: The Lake over the Water, Limitation. The superior man, in accordance with this, establishes measures and standards, and discusses virtue and conduct.

61.name: Zhong Fu

lines.meaning: Being prepared and at ease brings good fortune. If there are other thoughts, one becomes restless. — A settled mind brings good fortune.

lines.text: Crying crane in shade - young answers.
lines.meaning: A crane calls in the shade, its young answer. I have a fine goblet of wine, I will share it with you. — Sincerity resonates, like minds unite.

lines.text: Meeting rival - drum then stop, weep then sing.
lines.meaning: Meeting an opponent, now beating the drum, now stopping, now weeping, now singing. — An unsettled mind leads to erratic words and actions.

lines.text: Moon nearly full - horse vanishes. No blame.
lines.meaning: The moon nearly full, the horse is lost, no blame. — Not seeking perfection brings no fault.
Bound closely with sincerity, no blame. — The cohesive power of sincerity.
A bird flies high, its cry reaches heaven. Perseverance brings danger. — Fame exceeding reality brings peril.

61.xiang: The Wind over the Lake, Inner Truth. The superior man, in accordance with this, discusses legal cases and delays executions.

62.name: Xiao Guo

lines.meaning: A bird brings omens of misfortune. — In small excess, striving upward when one should not brings evil.
Passing over the grandfather, meeting the grandmother. Not reaching the ruler, meeting the minister, no blame. — In small excess, one finds encounters.
No excess, but be on guard. Indulgence may lead to harm, misfortune. — Be vigilant in small excess.
No blame, meeting without excess. Going forward is dangerous, one must be cautious, not stubbornly clinging to the norm.

lines.text: Dense clouds, no rain - duke shoots.
lines.meaning: Dense clouds but no rain, from the western outskirts. The duke shoots an arrow, taking prey in a cave. — Insufficient power to act freely.

lines.text: No meeting - excessive. Flying bird trapped.
lines.meaning: Meeting no one, rushing forward excessively. The bird is caught in a net, misfortune. This is disaster from heaven and man.

62.xiang: The Thunder on the Mountain, Small Excess. The superior man, in accordance with this, is excessively respectful in conduct, excessively sorrowful in mourning, and excessively frugal in expenditure.

63.name: Ji Ji

lines.text: Drag wheels - wet tail. No blame.
lines.meaning: Dragging the wheels, wetting the tail, no blame. — Caution at the beginning of success.

lines.text: Woman loses curtain - returns in seven days.
lines.meaning: The woman loses her carriage ornament. Do not seek it; after seven days, it will be found. — Small losses need not be worried about.

lines.text: King attacks demon country - three years.
lines.meaning: The High Ancestor attacks the Demon Region, taking three years to conquer. Do not employ petty men. — Success is hard-won.

lines.text: Fine clothes become rags - careful all day.
lines.meaning: Having old clothes to mend leaks, be on guard all day. — Be vigilant in times of peace.

The eastern neighbor slaughters an ox for a grand sacrifice, but it is not as good as the western neighbor's simple offering with sincerity, which truly receives blessings. — Sincerity outweighs form.
Wetting the head, danger. — Success leads to peril if one becomes complacent.

63.xiang: The Water over the Fire, After Completion. The superior man, in accordance with this, thinks of danger and takes precautions against it.

64.name: Wei Ji

lines.meaning: The young fox wets its tail crossing the river, there is difficulty. — Starting but not finishing leads to failure.
Dragging the wheels, perseverance brings good fortune. — Acting steadily when not yet successful.
Before completion, rushing forward brings misfortune. But crossing the great river is favorable. — In incompletion, one must take risks to succeed.
Perseverance brings good fortune, remorse vanishes. Rouse yourself to attack the Demon Region, after three years receive reward from the great state. — Persistence brings achievement.
Perseverance brings good fortune, no regret. The light of the superior man, with sincerity, good fortune. — Virtue shines, sincerity moves.

lines.text: Sincere feasting - no blame. Wetting head loses all.
lines.meaning: Maintaining sincerity while drinking, no blame. But if drinking excessively, like wetting the head, even sincerity loses the right path. — Enjoyment must have limits.

64.xiang: The Fire over the Water, Before Completion. The superior man, in accordance with this, carefully distinguishes things and places them in their proper positions.
